28: dounai ten psychen autou lytron anti pollon — to give his soul as a ransom in exchange for many — lytron (ransom price) is the price paid to release a slave or captive; the LXX uses it for the redemption-payment. Anti (in exchange for, instead of) is a substitutionary preposition: the soul of Jesus stands in the place of the many. Psyche here means life/person-as-a-whole; Jesus gives himself entirely as the exchange-price. The Isaianic Servant background (Isaiah 53:10-12) provides the theological content: the Servant makes his soul an offering for sin and thereby justifies many.
1: hōmoíōthē gàr hē basileía tôn ouranôn anthrṓpō̧ oikodespótȩ̄ — for the kingdom of heaven is like a householder — Oikodespótēs (householder, master of the house) = the estate owner who has authority over household and workers. Hóstis exêlthen hama prōí (who went out early in the morning) — hama prōí = at dawn, the first of four hiring times in the parable.
2: symphōnḗsas dè metà tôn ergatôn ek dēnaríou tḕn hēméran — having agreed with the workers for a denarius per day — Symphōnéō (to agree, be in accord) is the contract verb. One denarius per day was standard daily wage; the agreement is explicit and fair. This sets up the legal baseline from which the parable will depart.
3: állous hestôtas en tȩ̂ agorâ̧ argous — others standing in the marketplace idle — Argoús (idle, unemployed — from a- + érgon = without work) describes unemployed day laborers waiting to be hired. The agora (marketplace/forum) was the hiring plaza; this is realistic depiction of ancient labor practices.
4: hó eàn ȩ̂ díkaion dṓsō hymîn — whatever is just I will give you — The second group receives no fixed-sum contract, only the promise of what is díkaion (just, right). This ambiguity is deliberate; the parable turns on what díkaion means from the owner’s perspective.
5: péktēn kaì enátēn — the sixth and ninth hours — Sixth hour = noon, ninth hour = 3 PM. Two more hiring rounds; still no fixed sum promised, only the owner’s just determination.
6: perì dè tḕn hendekátēn — about the eleventh hour — One hour before end of working day (twelfth hour = sunset). Tí hestékate hólēn tḕn hēméran argoí (why have you stood the whole day idle?) — the owner’s question highlights their day-long unemployment, not laziness; they answer hóti oudeìs hēmâs emisthṓsato (no one hired us).
7: hypágete kaì hymeîs eis tòn ampelôna — go, you also, into the vineyard — No wage specified at all for this last group. The parable withholds any contract with the eleventh-hour workers, forcing the ambiguity: what will they receive? The reader’s expectation mirrors the early workers’ reasonable assumption.
8: arxámenos apò tôn eschátōn héōs tôn prṓtōn — beginning from the last to the first — The reversal of payment order (arxámenos apò tôn eschátōn) is a Matthean literary device to create the conflict: the first workers see the last receive a full denarius and expect more. Epítropos (manager/steward) carries out the owner’s instructions.
9: élabon anà dēnárion — they received a denarius each — Aná (distributive: each) stresses individual equal payment. The eleventh-hour workers receive a full day’s wage for one hour’s work — the operative category is need/grace, not merit/proportion.
10: enómisan hóti pleîon lḗmpsontai — they supposed they would receive more — Nomízō (to suppose, consider customary) + future indicative lḗmpsontai = their expectation was reasonable by any merit-based logic. The aorist élabon kaì autoì anà dēnárion (they also received a denarius) delivers the shock.
11: egóggyzōn katà toû oikodespótou — grumbling against the householder — Goggýzō (to grumble, murmur) echoes LXX Israel’s murmuring in the wilderness (Ex Matthew 16:7-8; Numbers 14:2). The imperfect egóggyzōn suggests continuous complaining.
12: mían hṓran epoíēsan — they worked one hour — The early workers’ complaint highlights duration (mían hṓran vs. tò báros tês hēméras kaì tòn kaúsōna = the burden of the day and the scorching heat). Kaúsōn = burning heat, the Mediterranean afternoon heat blast; a legitimate hardship claim.
13: hetaîre — friend — Hetaîros (companion, associate) is a less intimate address than phílos (dear friend); it carries a slight distancing — the same term Jesus uses to Judas in Matthew 26:50. Ouk adikô̧ se (I am not wronging you) — present indicative, strong denial of injustice.
14: árōn tò sòn kaì hýpage — take what is yours and go — The imperative sequence árōn... hýpage (take... go) is a legal dismissal: the agreed contract is fulfilled. Toutô̧ dè tô̧ eschátō̧ doûnai hōs kaì soí (but to give this last one the same as you) — the owner’s sovereign prerogative is the theological key.
15: ouk éxestín moi hò thélō poiêsai en toîs emoîs — is it not lawful for me to do what I wish with what is mine? — Éxestin (it is permitted/lawful) frames the question in terms of property rights. Hò thélō (what I wish) asserts the owner’s sovereign freedom over his own property. Ho ophthalmos sou ponērós estin hóti egṑ agathós eimi (is your eye evil because I am good?) — the pōonēròs ophthalmos (evil eye) idiom = envy/stinginess (cf. Deuteronomy 15:9 LXX); to have an evil eye toward another’s good fortune.
16: éschatoi prôtoi kaì prôtoi éschatoi — last first and first last — The reversional formula (also Matthew 19:30) forms an inclusio around the parable. This is not a statement about who enters the kingdom but about the reordering of expectation: merit-based hierarchies are overturned by sovereign grace.
17: anabáinōn dè ho Iēsoûs eis Hierosólyma — going up to Jerusalem — Anabáinō (to go up) is the standard verb for ascent to Jerusalem (both geographical and cultic elevation). Kat' idían (privately, apart) indicates a separate instruction for the Twelve from the crowd, signaling the gravity of what follows.
18: idoù anabáinomen eis Hierosólyma — behold, we go up to Jerusalem — Third passion prediction (cf. Matthew 16:21; Matthew 17:22-23). Paradothḗsetai (will be handed over) — divine passive, future indicative. Toîs archierêusin kaì grammateûsin (to the chief priests and scribes) — the Jerusalem establishment, not Roman authorities, initiate the process.
19: paradṓsousin autòn toîs éthnesin — they will hand him over to the Gentiles — The specification toîs éthnesin (to the Gentiles/nations) adds new detail over the 16:21 prediction: Roman involvement now specified. The four verbs — empaîxai (mock), mastigôsai (flog), staurôsai (crucify), egertheî (raised) — provide a precise preview of the Passion sequence, with the resurrection as the final action.
20: prosêlthen autô̧ hē mḗtēr tôn huiôn Zebedaíou — the mother of the sons of Zebedee came to him — Matthew substitutes the mother for James and John themselves (cf. Mark 10:35). Proskynoûsa (worshiping/bowing) signals the request is presented with reverence. Aiteî ti ap' autoû (asking something from him) — the indefinite ti before specifying shows she knows the request is presumptuous.
21: eipè hína kathísōsin houtoi hoi dýo huioí mou — say that these two sons of mine may sit — Kathísōsin (aorist subjunctive of kathízō) in a hína + subjunctive request clause. The ek dexiôn sou kaì ex euōnýmōn (right and left) of the Messianic king recall the enthronement psalm idiom; these are positions of highest co-regency in ancient courts.
22: tò potḗrion hò egṑ méllō píein — the cup that I am about to drink — Potḗrion (cup) as a metaphor for divinely-appointed suffering echoes Psalms 75:8; Isaiah 51:17; Jeremiah 25:15-16. Méllō + infinitive = imminent future. Dýnasthe (are you able?) — present indicative question probing their capacity. Their confident dynámetha (we are able) is not rebuked but redirected.
23: tò mèn potḗrión mou píesthe — my cup you will indeed drink — The mén... dé contrast: yes they will share the cup (prophesying their martyrdom), but the eschatological seating is not Jesus’ to grant. Hoîs hētoimastai hypò toû patrós mou (for whom it has been prepared by my Father) — perfect passive hētoimastai: God has already determined eschatological positions; they are not up for dispensation.
24: ēganáktēsan perì tôn dýo adelphôn — they were indignant concerning the two brothers — Aganaktéō (to be indignant, angered) is a strong emotional verb; the ten’s anger reveals that they themselves coveted the positions. The request exposed ambition latent in the entire group.
25: oi árchontes tôn ethnôn katakyrieuousin autôn — the rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them — Katakyrieuō (to exercise dominion over, lord over) uses the kata- prefix for intensive, downward exercise of power. Katexousiázousin (exercise authority over) pairs with it: both verbs describe hierarchical domination as the Gentile model. Ou houtōs éstai en hymîn (it shall not be so among you) — future of prohibition.
26: hós eàn thélȩ̄ en hymîn mégas genésthai — whoever wishes to become great among you — Mégas (great) and prôtos (first) are the status-markers just sought. Éstai hymôn diákonos (shall be your servant) — diákonos (table-servant, deacon) is a role of active service, not honorary title. The future indicative éstai makes this a descriptive statement of the kingdom order, not merely moral advice.
27: hós eàn thélȩ̄ en hymîn eînai prôtos — whoever wishes to be first among you — Escalation from mégas (great, Matthew 20:26) to prôtos (first) matched by escalation from diákonos (servant) to doûlos (slave). Doûlos = bond-slave, the lowest social rank; the trajectory of greatness runs exactly opposite to social expectation.
29: ekporeuoménōn autôn apò Hierichoû — as they were going out from Jericho — Note the Synoptic variation: Mark 10:46 and Luke 18:35 place the healing at the entrance to Jericho. Matthew has two blind men (not one as in Mark/Luke); multiplication of witnesses is a Matthean pattern (cf. two demoniacs, Matthew 8:28).
30: eleêsón hēmâs, kýrie, huiòs Dauid — have mercy on us, Lord, Son of David — Eleéō (to have mercy, show compassion) is the LXX verb for divine compassion (hesed). The vocative huiè Dauid (Son of David) is the Messianic title used by those outside the inner circle who perceive Jesus’ identity. Ékraxan légontes (cried out saying) — krázō suggests urgent, loud crying.
31: epetímēsan autoîs ho óchlos — the crowd rebuked them — Epitimáō (to rebuke) is the same verb used for silencing demons; the crowd’s attempt to silence the blind men echoes the disciples’ rebuke of the children (Matthew 19:13). Meîzon ékraxan (they cried out all the more) — comparative: the louder opposition drives louder appeal.
32: stàs ho Iēsoûs ephṓnēsen autoús — Jesus stopped and called them — Stás (aorist participle of histēmi = having stopped) marks deliberate halt. Ephṓnēsen (called, summoned) — Jesus hears, stops, and calls them rather than going to them, requiring the exercise of their faith in approaching.
33: tí thélете poiḗsō hymîn — what do you wish I should do for you? — Verbatim parallel to the question to the Zebedee sons’ mother (Matthew 20:21), creating a deliberate contrast: she asked for thrones; the blind men ask only for sight. Anoigôsin hoi ophthalmoì hēmôn (that our eyes may be opened) — the humble petition for basic restoration.
34: splanchnistheìs dè ho Iēsoûs — moved with compassion Jesus — Splagchnízomai (visceral compassion, from splágchna = inner organs) recurs as the characteristic response of Jesus to human need (Matthew 9:36; Matthew 14:14; Matthew 15:32; Matthew 18:27). Hḗpsato tôn ommátōn autôn (touched their eyes) — háptomai + genitive; Jesus touches the specific affected organ. Anéblepsan (they saw again, recovered sight) and ēkolúthēsan autô̧ (followed him) — sight leads immediately to discipleship, the Matthean pattern of healing as call.
